html:
Al-Awza'i: the ransom-or-release verse (47:4) was abrogated by "kill them wherever you find them" (2:191) Abrogation Warfare & Jihad Prophetic Character Strong Tirmidhi #1612
"Al-Awza'i said: 'It has been conveyed to me that this Ayah is abrogated: Thereafter (is the time) either for generosity (to free them without ransom) or ransom (47:4). It was abrogated by: Kill them wherever you find them (2:191).'"

What the hadith says

Al-Awza'i (d. 774 CE), founder of his own legal school and one of the most respected early jurists, transmitted that Q 47:4 — the verse Muslim apologetics most commonly cites to show Islam permits releasing captives — is no longer in legal force. The abrogating verse is Q 2:191, "kill them wherever you find them." Tirmidhi preserves this immediately after the Badr-captives narration, which records Gabriel warning Muhammad that ransoming captives would cost Muslim lives at the next battle.

Why this is a problem

  1. The "mercy" verse apologetics relies on is claimed abrogated by the "kill" verse. Q 47:4 is the standard citation for Islamic war ethics permitting captive release. Al-Awza'i — not a fringe figure but a founding Sunni jurist — declares it no longer operative law, superseded by a verse mandating killing.
  2. The substitution moves from leniency to lethality. The abrogation replaces a verse giving rulers discretion (release, ransom, enslave, or execute) with a verse commanding killing. The doctrinal direction is the opposite of moral progress.
  3. Tirmidhi's context endorses the harsher view. The compiler appends Ahmad ibn Hanbal's "if they kill, I see no harm" and Ishaq ibn Rahawayh's "wiping them out is better to me." Three canonical authorities, all favouring killing over mercy.
  4. The Badr-captives narration frames ransom as Muhammad's mistake. The adjacent hadith records Gabriel warning that ransoming the Badr prisoners would cost Muslim lives at Uhud. The two reports together build a legal case against clemency toward captives — the abrogation preventing future rulers from repeating the error.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics distinguishes al-Awza'i's individual opinion from binding scholarly consensus. Many scholars — including Ibn Abbas, al-Shafi'i, and most Hanafis — hold Q 47:4 unabrogated. The ruler retains discretion to ransom, release, enslave, or execute. Al-Awza'i's note is a minority juristic position preserved for completeness, not majority law.

Why it fails

The "merely one opinion" defense concedes that mainstream early jurisprudence debated the abrogation seriously enough to require refutation. Tirmidhi pairs al-Awza'i's view with Ahmad ibn Hanbal's preference for killing captives and Ishaq's "wipe them out" comment — three of the most-cited classical authorities all inclining toward the harsher reading. Whatever the ultimate consensus, the canonical record preserves a body of authoritative Muslim jurisprudence in which the merciful verse was held abrogated by the lethal one. The text is part of the operational tradition, not a historical curiosity.

Kill both the active and passive partner in a homosexual act — Tirmidhi's version Treatment of Disbelievers Prophetic Character Strong Tirmidhi #1456
"Whoever you find committing the act of the people of Lut, kill the one who does it and the one to whom it is done."

What the hadith says

Tirmidhi preserves the capital-punishment ruling for homosexual acts. Both partners are to be killed. Tirmidhi himself graded the chain Hasan.

Why this is a problem

  1. Cross-collection preservation strengthens its legal weight. Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah all carry this death-penalty ruling. Classical Islamic law draws on the convergence across collections when building case for a ruling.
  2. Bukhari and Muslim — the most authoritative collections — omit it. Classical jurists noticed the sahihayn omission; they proceeded with death penalties anyway, treating the convergent evidence as sufficient.
  3. The ruling does not distinguish coercion from consent. "The one to whom it is done" can include a coerced party. The hadith mandates execution regardless of whether the act was voluntary.
  4. Six Muslim-majority jurisdictions currently impose death for same-sex acts. Iran, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Brunei, northern Nigeria, and parts of Somalia draw direct theological cover from this hadith and its parallels. The text is not archaeological; it is operational law with living victims.

The Muslim response

Apologists cite the Hasan (rather than Sahih) grade as permitting critical distance, and note classical jurisprudence varied on exact penalty — the Hanafi school traditionally prescribed discretionary punishment rather than death, while the near-impossible four-witness evidentiary standard makes conviction practically unachievable in any case. The Quran itself supplies no specific criminal penalty for homosexual acts.

Why it fails

The Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali schools — and later Hanafi positions — all codified death as the penalty, which is why states applying classical jurisprudence still execute for same-sex acts today. The hadith's function was precisely to supply the death penalty the Quran does not supply, and it has served that function for 1,400 years. The observation that the Quran is silent is a problem rather than a defense: it means capital punishment for consensual private adult intimacy rests on a sub-Sahih hadith — and that is the warrant on which people are executed today.

"My ummah will split into 73 sects — all in the Fire except one" Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Tirmidhi #2641
"The Jews split into 71 sects, the Christians split into 72 sects, and my nation will split into 73 sects — all of them in the Fire except one." They said: "Who are they, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "Those who are upon what I and my Companions are upon today."

What the hadith says

Muhammad predicted his community would fracture into 73 sects. Only one would be saved. The saved group is identified as those following Muhammad and his companions' current practice.

Why this is a problem

  1. 72 out of 73 Muslim groupings are damned. Sunni, Shia, Ibadi, Sufi, Ahmadiyya, Ismaili, Alawite, Druze, and every madhhab — the hadith's arithmetic condemns all but one to the Fire. The one is never specifically named.
  2. Every Muslim group claims to be the saved sect. Sunnis, Shia, Wahhabis, Salafis, and Sufis all cite this hadith to anathematise their rivals. It has functioned as a permanent weapon for mutual takfir across every sectarian conflict in Islamic history.
  3. The numerical escalation (71 → 72 → 73) is rhetorical pattern, not prophecy. Each Abrahamic religion one more sect than the previous. This reads as narrative convention, not divinely foreknown arithmetic.
  4. It contradicts the Quran's "one ummah" theology. Q 21:92 insists the Muslim community is a single entity. The hadith then divides it into 73 fragments, 72 of which go to hell. The contradiction is unresolved.

The Muslim response

Apologists argue the hadith is a warning, not a condemnation: it urges unity in adherence to the Quran and Sunnah, with the "saved sect" criterion being behavioural (fidelity to prophetic practice) rather than denominational. The 73-sect arithmetic is rhetorical rather than precise; individuals from multiple communities can qualify by meeting the behavioural standard.

Why it fails

The "behavioural criterion" reading does not prevent every Sunni, Shia, Salafi, and Ahmadiyya community from simultaneously claiming to be the saved sect — each believes its practice matches the Prophet's. The hadith's structural effect is mutual excommunication: every Muslim subcommunity reads the other subcommunities as the damned 72. A hadith whose primary operational function is enabling takfir of other Muslims has not preserved unity; it has institutionalised its permanent destruction.

Every martyr receives 72 virgin wives in paradise Women Jesus / Christology Strange / Obscure Strong Tirmidhi #2687 (and parallel #1663)
"The martyr has six special favors with Allah: he is forgiven with the first drop of his blood; he is shown his seat in paradise; he is protected from the torment of the grave; he is secure from the greatest terror; a crown of dignity is placed on his head; he is married to seventy-two Hur al-'Ayn (wide-eyed virgins); and his intercession is accepted for seventy of his relatives."

What the hadith says

Tirmidhi specifies six martyrdom rewards, including marriage to 72 wide-eyed virgin houris and intercession for 70 relatives.

Why this is a problem

  1. The precise "72" figure originates here. Bukhari and Muslim have houris but not the specific count. Tirmidhi supplies the number that modern jihadist recruitment uses. The propaganda's imagery has a specific canonical source.
  2. The reward is explicitly sexual. Paradise reward for dying in battle is virgin wives. The afterlife is imagined as a harem specifically calibrated for young men willing to die fighting.
  3. The gender asymmetry is structural, not incidental. Male martyrs get 72 wives. Female martyrs receive no corresponding sexual reward. The design reveals the intended audience.
  4. It has directly fuelled terror recruitment. ISIS, al-Qaeda, and Hamas have cited the 72-virgin promise in recruiting materials. The number comes from this text. The connection between canonical content and operational violence is direct.

The Muslim response

Apologists cite the Hasan grade (not Sahih) and argue the houri language is metaphorical across Quranic passages. Some scholars invoke the Luxenberg "white grapes" thesis. Mainstream apologetics frames paradise-reward language as figurative incentive toward righteousness, not literal sexual description.

Why it fails

The Hasan grade is authoritative in mainstream Sunni jurisprudence; it entered Islamic paradise theology through exactly this channel. Modern jihadist recruitment uses the number specifically, not metaphorically. The Luxenberg thesis is a fringe philological speculation rejected by both Muslim and non-Muslim Quranic scholarship. The combined Quran-plus-hadith corpus uses unmistakably sexual language (large-eyed, well-guarded, maidens of equal age, untouched by jinn or human) that no metaphor-reading fits without deeply rewriting the texts. The gender asymmetry — specific sexual reward for men, nothing comparable for women — marks a reward system designed for one sex.

A surah once as long as al-Baqarah — now reduced to a single verse Contradiction Logical Inconsistency Strong Tirmidhi #2536 (parallel commentary); classical tafsir
[Ubayy bin Ka'b and Aisha narrations preserved:] "A verse on stoning was revealed. We used to recite it. Now it is gone." / "Surah al-Ahzab was once 200 verses. Now it is 73."

What the hadith says

Multiple companion testimonies in Tirmidhi and parallel collections record that the Quran once contained passages now absent: a stoning verse, a longer Surah al-Ahzab (73 verses, was once ~200), and other lost material. The witnesses are Aisha, Ubayy bin Ka'b, and Umar — not hostile outsiders.

Why this is a problem

  1. It directly contradicts Q 15:9's preservation promise. "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder and indeed, We will be its guardian." The hadith corpus's own companion testimony undermines the Quran's central self-authentication claim.
  2. The witnesses are the most authoritative possible. Aisha (Muhammad's wife), Ubayy bin Ka'b (Muhammad's primary Quran-reciter), and Umar (second caliph) — the canonical record's most trusted transmitters are the ones confirming textual loss.
  3. Capital punishment law rests on a missing verse. The stoning penalty (rajm) for adultery has no surviving Quranic basis. Classical jurisprudence acknowledges the verse was there and is now gone — yet the death penalty it prescribed continues to be applied.
  4. Uthman's burning of variant codices explains the mechanism. The caliph's standardisation destroyed competing manuscripts. The tradition preserves both the preservation promise and the destruction event without resolving the tension.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics distinguishes naskh al-tilawa (abrogation of recitation) from naskh al-hukm (abrogation of ruling): some verses were withdrawn from recitation while their legal rulings persisted; others had both recitation and ruling withdrawn. The "lost" passages were intentionally withdrawn by Allah, not accidentally lost — so the preservation promise applies to what Allah intended to preserve.

Why it fails

The naskh al-tilawa doctrine is the theological management for a real textual problem — it was developed precisely to harmonise the companion testimonies of lost verses with the preservation claim. The distinction is not in the Quran; it was constructed afterward. More critically: if the stoning verse was "intentionally withdrawn," why does classical jurisprudence continue to apply its ruling on the basis of the very verse that was supposedly removed? The legal system acts as though the verse is authoritative; the preservation doctrine acts as though it was withdrawn. Both cannot be simultaneously true without special pleading.

"I have been made victorious through terror" Violence Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Tirmidhi #1553
"I have been made victorious through terror [cast into the hearts of my enemies]."

What the hadith says

Muhammad attributed his military victories to terror — specifically, divine fear cast into enemies' hearts at a month's journey's distance. The hadith is also preserved in Bukhari and Muslim.

Why this is a problem

  1. Terror is named as a divinely granted method of victory. Muhammad did not merely benefit from enemies' fear — he presented it as Allah's specific gift to him, unique among prophets. Modern jihadist groups cite this hadith directly as theological warrant. "We are made victorious through terror" is ISIS-era propaganda wording drawn from the canonical text.
  2. Q 8:60 authorises the same. "Prepare against them whatever you are able of power... to terrify the enemies of Allah." The hadith implements Quranic instruction; the two reinforce each other.
  3. The classical "terror at a month's journey" quantifies the scope. The fear extended well beyond the battlefield into the psychological sphere of civilian populations. This is not battlefield morale but population-level intimidation named as a divine military tool.
  4. Modern Muslim apologetics frequently omits this hadith. Its absence from popular presentations while it remains in the sahih record is the observable gap between the apologetic tradition and the canonical one.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames the hadith as describing supernatural psychological warfare unique to Muhammad's prophetic mission — fear cast into polytheist enemies' hearts by divine act, not as a model for human tactical terror. Modern teachers read it as a historical military advantage specific to the early Muslim community's exceptional circumstances.

Why it fails

The "unique to Muhammad's mission" reading does not prevent the hadith from being cited as operational warrant — which is precisely what jihadist movements do when they invoke it. If the terror was divinely granted and divinely approved in that context, the groups citing it as justification for modern political violence are making a straightforward analogical argument the text does not block. The canonical record names terror as a divinely sanctioned victory method. Whether that sanction was historical-only or eternal is a judgment the hadith does not make for the reader.

Aisha's age confirmed by Tirmidhi: married at six, consummated at nine Women Prophetic Character Strong Tirmidhi #1109
"The Messenger of Allah married 'Aishah when she was six years old, and consummated the marriage with her when she was nine."

What the hadith says

Tirmidhi preserves the Aisha age narrative — married at six, consummated at nine — as an independent chain adding to the cross-collection attestation.

Why this is a problem

  1. Five major Sunni collections independently confirm the ages. Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah all preserve the 6/9 report through separate chains. The convergent attestation is not a single tradition being copied; it is multiply-sourced agreement.
  2. Revisionist "Aisha was older" arguments require rejecting all five. The hadith-science methodology Sunni Islam uses to authenticate any other hadith cannot be selectively suspended for this one. Wholesale rejection of all five major collections on a single topic requires acknowledging a foundational problem with canonical authority.
  3. Muhammad as uswa hasana plus child-marriage permissibility produces real-world harm. Child marriage remains legal in several Muslim-majority countries partly on the precedent this text establishes. The canonical record is not inert; it is a live justification in ongoing legal debates.

The Muslim response

Standard apologetic responses include: physical maturity was the relevant criterion in 7th-century context; cultural norms differed universally in the ancient world; revisionist redating argues Aisha was closer to 18 based on alternative calculations from other hadiths about her sister Asma.

Why it fails

The cross-collection confirmation is precisely the problem for revisionist redating: rejecting the 6/9 ages requires dismissing Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, and Ibn Majah — the five most authoritative Sunni hadith collections — on the basis of indirect calculations from other hadiths while ignoring the direct testimony. If these five collections cannot be trusted on the Prophet's own marriage, the canonical apparatus is compromised across the board. The "cultural norms" defense makes no moral argument that the practice was good — only that it was common. The price for that concession is accepting that the Prophet modelled behaviour that was culturally normal rather than morally superior.

Banu Qurayza: the execution order for 600-900 Jewish men and enslavement of women Prophetic Character Violence Antisemitism Strong Tirmidhi classical commentary (#1582)
"Sa'd ibn Mu'adh said: 'The ruling is that their fighting men be killed, and their women and children enslaved.'"

What the hadith says

Tirmidhi's tafsir material preserves the Banu Qurayza episode: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh was appointed arbitrator for the Jewish tribe who had allegedly violated their treaty with Muhammad during the Battle of the Trench. Sa'd ruled that the men be executed and the women and children enslaved. Muhammad said Sa'd had judged by Allah's own ruling. Between 600 and 900 men were beheaded.

Why this is a problem

  1. Muhammad approved and facilitated a mass execution. The sa'd-as-arbitrator framing does not distance Muhammad from the outcome — he selected Sa'd as arbitrator, affirmed the verdict as divine, and presided over the executions.
  2. "Judged by Allah's own ruling" makes the massacre a divine act. Muhammad's validation elevated a tribal chieftain's revenge verdict into sacred law. Modern apologetics that describes this as Sa'd's independent judgment must contend with the Prophet's explicit divine endorsement.
  3. The scope of "fighting men" is contested. Classical sources indicate post-pubescent boys were checked for pubic hair and executed alongside adult men. The criterion was biological rather than strictly military.
  4. Modern apologetics attempt to limit scope; the sahih tradition is explicit. The beheadings, the ditch, the enslavement of women — these details are preserved in Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, and Tirmidhi without critique. The tradition does not express regret; it expresses divine sanction.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics argues the Banu Qurayza violated a treaty during wartime, making their punishment a matter of treason law rather than ethnic violence. Sa'd's ruling was consistent with the law of nations in that era. Modern teachers add that the event must be read in its 7th-century political context, not through anachronistic modern norms.

Why it fails

The "treaty violation" framing is disputed — the evidence of actual Banu Qurayza betrayal during the siege is thin, and the verdict was issued before a fair hearing. The "consistent with era norms" argument concedes the event was morally ordinary for the time, which directly contradicts the claim that Muhammad's conduct represented divine moral guidance above and beyond his era. If the massacre was what anyone in 7th-century Arabia would have done, it provides no evidence of prophetic moral elevation.

Allah's Throne rests on eight angelic goats above seven seas and heavens Science Claims Strange / Obscure Strong Tirmidhi #3320
"Above the seventh heaven is a sea. Between its highest part and its lowest is just as there is between one heaven to another heaven. Above that are eight goats, between their hooves and backs is the same as what is between one heaven and another heaven. Then above their backs is the Throne..."

What the hadith says

The cosmic architecture: seven heavens stacked at equal intervals; above them a sea; above the sea eight enormous goats (identified as angelic beings); on their backs, Allah's Throne; Allah above that.

Why this is a problem

  1. Eight angelic goats as cosmic load-bearers. Specific pastoral imagery is preserved as canonical cosmology. The goat-motif is inherited from Bronze Age herding cultures' cosmological imagination.
  2. Seas above the heavens reflects ancient Near Eastern cosmology. Genesis 1:7 ("the waters above the firmament") and Mesopotamian cosmic-ocean traditions share the same framework. The Tirmidhi hadith preserves a pre-scientific cosmological model common to the region's ancient religious literature.
  3. The "equal interval" arithmetic is formulaic, not measured. The consistent "500 years between" units recur across multiple cosmological hadiths. The number is symbolic rather than empirical.
  4. Tirmidhi himself grades it Hasan Gharib. Even the compiler flags this as rare-chain. Yet it circulates as canonical Islamic cosmology and appears in tafsir literature on Q 40:7 (the angels who carry the Throne).

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics reads the Throne, the sea, and the goats as symbolic expressions of divine majesty and transcendence rather than literal cosmological claims. The specific imagery conveys grandeur appropriate to 7th-century Arabic cultural expression. Modern Muslim theology generally treats Throne-cosmology hadiths as beyond precise human comprehension (bila kayf).

Why it fails

The bila kayf response is applied after the fact: the hadith presents specific, concrete, measurable details (goats with distance between hoof and back equal to the distance between heavens). This is not vague symbolic language; it is precise spatial description. Invoking incomprehensibility as interpretation requires abandoning the text's plain register. The cosmological framework the hadith preserves — layered heavens, celestial seas, animal throne-bearers — is the shared architecture of ancient Near Eastern religious cosmology, not a unique divine revelation. Its presence in the canonical record reflects cultural inheritance, not cosmological discovery.

"Do not lash your wife like a slave — you may lie with her at day's end" Women Prophetic Character Strong Tirmidhi #3343
"One of you should not lash his wife as a slave is lashed, for perhaps he will lay with her at the end of the day."

What the hadith says

Muhammad advised men not to beat their wives at slave-intensity — because they will sleep with them that night. The advice is not a prohibition on beating but a counsel about degree, with the man's sexual access as the operative reason.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Like a slave" presupposes slaves can be beaten at full intensity. The reform is: don't beat wives as hard as slaves. Slave-grade beating for female slaves is the unstated baseline the hadith does not disturb.
  2. The rationale is sexual convenience, not the wife's pain. "You will lie with her tonight" is the moderating concern — not her suffering. The man's ability to have sex is the reason to reduce the beating's severity. Her welfare is absent from the reasoning.
  3. Wife-beating remains the baseline, modified not prohibited. The instruction is not "do not beat your wife" but "do not beat your wife like this." Q 4:34's permission to beat disobedient wives is the Quranic context; the hadith addresses degree rather than permissibility.
  4. The parallel between wife and slave is explicit. The hadith draws the comparison directly: wives and slaves are in the same category for the purpose of physical correction — distinguished only by the husband's sexual interest in one and not the other.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames the hadith as a moral reform reducing domestic violence: Muhammad intervened to moderate a practice that existed and could not be immediately prohibited in the social context. The comparison to slaves is read as a practical illustration, not an endorsement of slave-beating. Modern teachers cite the hadith alongside Prophet's statements that "the best of you are those best to their wives."

Why it fails

A reform that says "reduce the severity because of your own interests" is not a moral reform — it is a pragmatic instruction. The moral content (her pain, her dignity) is absent from the reasoning. The "best to their wives" hadith sits alongside this one in the canonical record without resolution; the tradition preserves both without a rule for which governs. The comparison to slaves is not an incidental illustration; it is the hadith's structural frame, and the frame reveals the category both women and slaves occupy in the tradition's moral imagination.

A Muslim is not executed in retaliation for killing a non-Muslim Treatment of Disbelievers Strong Tirmidhi #1412, #1413
"A believer is not killed for a disbeliever."

What the hadith says

If a Muslim kills a non-Muslim, Islamic qisas (retaliatory execution) does not apply. The Muslim's life is not forfeited for the non-Muslim's life. Also preserved in Bukhari and Abu Dawud.

Why this is a problem

  1. Human life has a religion-based legal value. Muslim lives are fully protected by qisas. Non-Muslim lives are not. The asymmetry is not incidental; it is the explicit rule.
  2. All four Sunni schools apply the rule. Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali jurisprudence all preserve the no-qisas-for-kafir principle, varying only in edge cases involving People of the Book with dhimmi status. The no-retaliation rule is cross-school consensus, not a minority position.
  3. It remains operational in several Islamic legal systems. Iran and Pakistan implement versions of the rule; Saudi case law applies it. The hadith is not historical; it currently governs how homicide cases are adjudicated in jurisdictions using Islamic personal-status law.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames the rule as a feature of the dhimmi citizenship structure: non-Muslims lived under different contractual terms, with jizya payment exchanging for protection but not full civic equality. Modern reformists point to hadith-based arguments for extending qisas protections equally and note that most Muslim-majority jurisdictions now formally apply equal homicide law.

Why it fails

The "contractual structure" framing concedes that the canonical legal system encoded a tiered citizenship with different life-values as the baseline. Modern formal equality is a reform of the canonical position, not a recovery of it. Presenting it as "what Islam really teaches" requires ignoring a clear hadith preserved across multiple canonical collections and applied uniformly by all four Sunni law schools for fourteen centuries.

Do not have intercourse with a pregnant captive until she gives birth Women Prophetic Character Strong Tirmidhi #1131
"Do not have intercourse with a pregnant captive until she gives birth, nor with a non-pregnant one until she has one menstrual cycle."

What the hadith says

The hadith regulates sexual access to captive women: wait until delivery for pregnant captives, one menstrual cycle for others. The waiting period (istibra') confirms no prior pregnancy before the captor proceeds.

Why this is a problem

  1. The rule presupposes sexual access to captives as default. The instruction is about timing, not permission. Sex with captive women is baseline-legitimate; the hadith manages the procedure.
  2. The waiting period is a paternity concern, not a consent concern. The concern is to avoid impregnating a captive who is already pregnant — a property and inheritance question. There is no inquiry into whether the woman consents to sexual contact at all.
  3. ISIS cited hadiths like this for procedural guidance. The 2014 Sabaya Manual governing Yazidi captives drew on classical captive-sex regulations including the istibra' requirement. The hadith was used as the procedural rule governing the rape of enslaved women.

The Muslim response

Classical apologetics frames the istibra' rule as a protective regulation for captive women — ensuring paternity clarity and preventing immediate abuse. The rule demonstrates Islam's regulatory concern for captives' welfare within the institution. Modern Muslim teaching notes that slavery has been abolished and that these rules are historically contextualised.

Why it fails

A regulation that protects the captor's paternity interests while imposing no consent requirement on the captive is not protective of the captive's welfare — it is property management. The "historically contextualised" response concedes the rule was live law and cannot simultaneously present it as Islamic humanitarianism. The ISIS application demonstrates that "historical context" evaporates when the political conditions for enslaving captives re-emerge; the procedural hadith remains operational wherever the institution does.
